title: The Truth Table: Mapping Every Possibility
concept: A truth table lists every possible combination of true/false inputs for a gate, alongside the exact output the gate produces for each one.

exercises.prompt: Recap: A bank vault requiring a keycard AND a PIN AND a manager's approval is an example of...

options.text: Combining multiple simple gates into one bigger decision
A single gate with no other rules

exercises.explain: Exactly! And now: how do we check a gate does exactly what we expect, in every single situation? By mapping out every possibility.

cards.title: What if you missed a case?
cards.body: Imagine wiring an alarm system and being 99% sure it works... except for one input combination you never tested. In engineering, "probably works" isn't good enough — you need to check every possibility.

cards.title: What is a truth table?
cards.body: A truth table is a chart that lists every possible combination of true/false inputs to a gate, along with the exact output for each one. For 2 inputs, that's exactly 4 rows — no more, no fewer.

cards.title: A checklist with no gaps
cards.body: A truth table is like a restaurant checking every seating combination before opening night. Instead of hoping the tables all fit, they map out every scenario in advance — leaving nothing to chance.

cards.title: The AND gate's truth table
cards.body:
Input A, Input B → Output.
false, false → false
false, true → false
true, false → false
true, true → true
Only one row out of four turns out true — that's what "strict" looks like on paper.

cards.title: You don't need a computer to build one
cards.body: It's easy to assume truth tables are something only a computer generates. In reality, with 2 inputs there are only 4 rows — anyone can sketch a full truth table by hand in under a minute.

cards.title: AND's table vs. OR's table
cards.body: Same four rows, one row flips the whole story:

left.text:
True on only 1 of 4 rows
(both inputs true)

right.text:
True on 3 of 4 rows
(at least one input true)

exercises.prompt: How many rows does a truth table need for a gate with exactly 2 inputs?
exercises.explain: Right! Each input can independently be true or false, so 2 inputs give 2 × 2 = 4 possible combinations.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A NOT gate's truth table has only 2 rows, since it only has 1 input.
exercises.explain: True! With a single input, there are only two possibilities: true or false — so only 2 rows are needed.

exercises.sentence: A truth table lists every possible combination of ___ for a gate.
exercises.explain: A truth table's whole job is to enumerate every possible combination of inputs and show the resulting output.

exercises.prompt: A truth table shows: (false,false)→false, (false,true)→false, (true,false)→false. Which row is missing, and what must its output be for this to be an AND gate?
exercises.explain: Correct! An AND gate is true on exactly one row: when every input is true. The missing row must be (true, true) → true.

exercises.prompt: You're testing a new 2-input gate. You observe: (false,false)→false, (false,true)→true, (true,false)→true, (true,true)→true. Based on this truth table, which gate is this?
exercises.explain: Exactly! The output is true whenever at least one input is true — that's the signature of an OR gate. Next: let's ground all this in something physical — actual switches and circuits you could touch.
